markdown:
<callout type="note">The** important aspect** of **balance of payments** is the **interdependence** between the **accounts** (components) that is **measured**.</callout>

We will bring up the balance of payments example table for **Country A **from _4.6.2 Components of Balance of Payments._

| Line | Components | Figures (Millions of dollars) |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | Current Account |  |
| 2 | Exports of Goods | +25 |
| 3 | Imports of Goods | -40 |
| 4 | Balance of Trade in Goods (Line 2 - 3) | -25 |
| 5 | Exports of Services | +15 |
| 6 | Imports of Services | -5 |
| 7 | Balance of Trade in Services (Line 5 - 6) | +10 |
| 8 | Balance of Trade in Goods & Services (Line 4 + 7) | -15 |
| 9 | Income (inflows - outflows) | -4 |
| 10 | Current Transfers (inflows - outflows) | +1 |
| 11 | Balance on Current Account (Line 8 + 9 + 10) | -18 |
|  |  |  |
| 12 | Capital Account |  |
| 13 | Capital Transfers (inflows - outflows) | +1.1 |
| 14 | Transactions non-financial assets (inflows - outflows) | +0.9 |
| 15 | Balance on Capital Account (Line 13 + 14) | +2 |
|  |  |  |
| 16 | Financial Account |  |
| 17 | Foreign Direct Investment (inflows - outflows) (FDI; inflows - outflows) | +18 |
| 18 | Portfolio Investment (inflows - outflows) | -3 |
| 19 | Reserve Assets (official reserves) | +2 |
| 20 | Official Borrowing | -1 |
| 21 | Balance of Financial Account (Line 17 + 18 + 19 + 20) | +16 |
|  |  |  |
| 22 | Balance (Line 11 + 15 + 21) | 0 |

### Zero Balance in the Balance of Payments


1. In balance of payments, the **sum of all components is always 0.**
2. In other words, the sum of all credits and debits is 0, or** credits are matched by the debits**.
3. This can also be stated as **deficits are matched by the surpluses**.

<callout type="example">Observing in **Country A's** balance of payments accounts, the **sum of surplus** in the** capital** and **financial** account of +18 million USD is **exactly matched** by the **deficit** in the **current** account of -18 million USD.</callout>

### Reasoning behind Zero Balance


1. Recall, credits **refer** to the **payments received by **the **economy** from other economies and debits **refer **to the **payments made from** the economy.
2. For credits, when **other** **countries** are trying to **pay** Country A, they must **sell** their own **currency** and **demand** **Country A's currency**.
3. The same applies for payments made or debits by **Country A**, as they must **sell their own currency **and demand the **other** country's **currency**.
4. Therefore, **credits** **create demand for a currency **and **debits create supply of the currency**.

Now, going to the **topic** of exchange rates:

1. **Exchange rates** are always **determined** by the **demand and supply **of the **currency**, regardless of the exchange rate systems.
2. The **equilibrium** or the **point of intersection** of the **two curves is what the exchange rate **of the currency is.
3. For credits and debits, as they create** demand and supply** for the currency respectively, at the equilibrium point, the** sum of credits** is equal to the **sum of debits**.
4. In other words, **deficits are matched by the surpluses**.



### Imbalance in Balance of Payments


1. Even though the **balance of payments** must **always** balance, **deficit and surplus of **balance of payments are discussed frequently. 
2. **Balance of payments surplus **refers to the** surplus **in the combined current, capital, and financial accounts (plus errors and omissions), **_excluding central bank intervention (reserve assets)_**.
3. **Meanwhile, balance of payments deficit **refers to the** deficit **in the combined current, capital, and financial accounts (plus errors and omissions), **_excluding central bank intervention (reserve assets)_**.
4. As the balance of payments **must always balance**, if there is a **deficit or surplus**, the central bank **intervenes** by **purchasing or supplying** the currency to make it **balanced.**
5. The balance can be **achieved **through **government intervention** or by** market forces,** which is discussed later.

<callout type="example">**Country A** has a balance of payments deficit as if we **exclude** the **reserve** **assets** of (+2 million):

$\text{Current Account Deficit} (-18)+$ $ \text{Capital Account Surplus} (+2) +$  $\text{Financial Account Surplus, excluding intervention} (+14) = $  $\text{Balance of Payments Deficit} (-2)$</callout>
